reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Employer- and labour-market literature (e.g. OECD Skills Outlook, Manpower/ManpowerGroup Talent Shortage surveys, IVSZ digital-skills reports) consistently documents two employer priorities: (1) reliable early identification of high-ability students who can be channelled into STEM/digital-intensive tracks, and (2) predictability of the admissions pipeline so firms and training providers can plan multi-year recruitment and apprenticeship pipelines.
reactions.interest: Employers and the digital/tech sector as consumers of the future skilled-labour pipeline, including firms that recruit STEM and digitally-skilled graduates and that co-invest in school partnerships, scholarships and apprenticeships tied to selective tracks
reactions.public_good_frame: A talent pipeline that selects on genuine ability rather than purchased test-prep is a more efficient allocator of scarce STEM/digital talent for the national economy, reducing the waste of ability that goes uncultivated because of family income rather than aptitude
reactions.argument: A less coachable assessment component could improve the signal value of admission (ability rather than prep-purchasing power), which serves the digital economy's interest in a deeper and more meritocratic STEM talent pool than the current prep-course-sensitive exam provides; SES quotas are acceptable to this interest only insofar as they do not lower the average preparedness of the intake, since employers and downstream STEM education providers rely on the selective track's output quality as a signal. The multi-year psychometric validation and administrative uncertainty flagged in the scenario are a real cost to this interest because firms and training institutions plan multi-year pipelines around predictable admission criteria.
reactions.condition_to_change: Would move to opposition if the redesigned assessment or quota parameters measurably lowered the academic preparedness of the resulting selective-track cohort, or if the multi-year pilot-and-rollout uncertainty (2-3 admission cycles plus compliance-audit buildup) created recruitment-pipeline unpredictability lasting beyond the stated timeline

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Same labour-market basis as S1 (OECD Skills Outlook, employer talent-shortage surveys): employers value both a large, broad-based skilled-labour pool and a credible pipeline for identifying and cultivating high-ability specialists; they also assign a real cost to policy volatility (multi-year hiring and training pipelines depreciate in value if the underlying schooling structure they are built on can be reversed by the next government, as the scenario's own political-risk section notes was the case in Poland).
reactions.interest: Employers and the digital/tech sector, particularly firms and training providers whose STEM/digital talent pipelines and apprenticeship partnerships are built around the existing selective-track structure
reactions.public_good_frame: A broader base of well-prepared general-school graduates expands the addressable pool for the digitalising economy's demand for numerate, digitally literate workers beyond a small elite, provided the shrinking selective track does not become the only route to advanced STEM preparation
reactions.argument: This interest is not opposed in principle to shrinking selective-entry share, since a wider base of digitally competent school-leavers benefits mass employer demand (not just elite recruiters); but the scenario's own uncertainty section flags that general-school absorption of high-achievers is a 'contested' and unevidenced mechanism, and the digital sector depends on that mechanism actually working to avoid a net loss of advanced STEM talent output. Reversal risk documented in the scenario's political-risk section (the Polish precedent) is itself a direct cost to this interest, since firms that build training partnerships around a phased trajectory lose value if the trajectory is frozen mid-course.
reactions.condition_to_change: Would support unconditionally if pilot evaluations (as proposed in the scenario's own uncertainty-reduction steps) demonstrate that funded general-school advanced programmes replicate STEM/digital preparation comparable to current selective tracks, and would oppose if evidence instead shows a net contraction in advanced-track output or if the entrenchment mechanism fails to prevent reversal

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Labour-market literature on comprehensive versus early-tracking systems (cross-country PISA/Hanushek-Woessmann-type evidence cited in the scenario itself) is generally read by employer and workforce-development bodies as favouring broad-based average skill levels for a digitalising economy where most jobs require moderate-to-high but not elite numeracy/digital literacy; separately, employer bodies consistently flag teacher supply as a binding constraint on any reform's credibility (per OECD Education at a Glance-type teacher-shortage data referenced elsewhere in this analysis).
reactions.interest: Employers and the digital/tech sector as consumers of both a broad base of digitally literate workers and a smaller specialist STEM pipeline, and as parties exposed to multi-year uncertainty during a decade-long transition
reactions.public_good_frame: Postponing academic sorting to 14 could, if the retraining and support-system bundle succeeds, raise average workforce digital/numeracy competence across the whole economy rather than concentrating it in a shrinking elite track — but only if implementation avoids the disruption and reversal risk the scenario itself documents
reactions.argument: This interest has no principled objection to later tracking as such, since Nordic-style comprehensive systems are associated with strong average skill outcomes valuable to a digitalising economy that needs broad-based competence, not only a narrow elite. However, the scenario's own uncertainties concede that Hungary's teacher shortage — a constraint this interest already experiences directly through its own recruitment difficulties for technically qualified staff — could undermine the differentiated-instruction retraining the reform depends on, and the 6-8 year implementation horizon with an unresolved fallback plan (per the scenario's own political-risk section) creates exactly the kind of multi-year policy uncertainty that discourages employer co-investment in school partnerships and apprenticeship pipelines.
reactions.condition_to_change: Would move toward support if pilot-district evaluations show successful teacher retraining and no measurable loss in advanced-track STEM output, and toward opposition if teacher-shortage constraints or a failed cross-party pact leave the reform half-implemented or subject to reversal, stranding the transition-cohort investment

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Employer and workforce-development literature generally treats policy stability and continuity of the existing selective pipeline as low-risk for near-term recruitment planning (per the scenario's own 'no structural change occurs' framing), while also documenting employer demand for rising baseline digital/STEM competence across the whole graduate population, not only the selective track (OECD Skills Outlook-type findings).
reactions.interest: Employers and the digital/tech sector, both those recruiting from the existing selective-track pipeline (who benefit from zero disruption) and those recruiting more broadly from general-school leavers (who would benefit from raised general-school standards)
reactions.public_good_frame: Preserving the selective pipeline's output while raising the floor in general schools serves the digital economy's dual need for a specialist talent stream and a broader digitally competent workforce, provided the compensation package is durable enough to actually raise skill levels rather than being cut in the next fiscal downturn
reactions.argument: This interest favours the lowest-disruption option in the near term, since it protects an already-functioning STEM/digital talent pipeline without transition costs. Its support for the compensation package itself is conditional, however, on whether curriculum standardisation genuinely raises digital/numeracy competence in general schools — the scenario's own uncertainties note that no comparator country has shown compensation fully offsetting selection effects at this age, and its own political-risk section flags compensation budgets as the first line item cut in fiscal consolidation, and Hungary's teacher shortage (evidenced strongly per OECD data cited in the scenario) directly limits how much curriculum standards alone can raise actual skill output regardless of funding intent.
reactions.condition_to_change: Would move to stronger support if the protected multi-year budget line proves durable across at least one fiscal downturn and general-school digital/STEM outcomes measurably rise; would move to opposition of the compensation-only approach if funding is cut in the first consolidation cycle, since that would leave the structure unchanged while foreclosing this interest's stated preference for a growing broad-based digital skills floor
